mnemonics: G-S-E: General boundary Starts with the most General hypothesis (S is the set of most Specific hypotheses).
Version Space is the 'Goldilocks Zone' of ML – it contains all hypotheses that are 'just right' for the training data, not too general (false positives) and not too specific (false negatives).
keyPoints: A Version Space is the set of all hypotheses consistent with the training examples.
The Candidate-Elimination algorithm iteratively adjusts the most general (G) and most specific (S) boundaries of the version space.
The algorithm converges when the G and S sets converge to an identical hypothesis, meaning the target concept is learned.
commonMistakes: Assuming the version space will always converge to a single hypothesis; it can represent multiple hypotheses consistent with the data.
Forgetting that the algorithm is sensitive to the order of training examples and noise in the data.
Confusing the general boundary (G) with the specific boundary (S); G is a set of the most general hypotheses, S is a set of the most specific.
